case_name: Agricultural reuse of mixed industrial-municipal wastewater with cyanide, chlorinated solvent legacy impacts, and cobalt under uncertain influent composition
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for treating mixed industrial-municipal wastewater to agricultural reuse quality under uncertain contaminant variability, mixed organic-inorganic pollution, and evolving compliance expectations.

input_data.scenario_background: A peri-urban utility receives municipal wastewater blended with intermittent industrial contributions from metal finishing, electroplating, and a legacy solvent-impacted drainage area. Cyanide and cobalt are periodically detected, while cis-1,2-dichloroethylene appears episodically after rainfall and groundwater intrusion, suggesting a partially characterized legacy plume rather than a steady source. The irrigation district wants reuse water for non-potable agricultural application, but regulators have signaled that future scrutiny may increase for trace organics and metal accumulation in soils. The exact peak concentrations and presence of related chlorinated VOCs are not fully known, so treatment must be robust to uncertainty rather than optimized only for average conditions.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Produce reclaimed water suitable for agricultural reuse with reliable reduction of pathogens, suspended solids, nitrogen-related impacts, cyanide, cobalt, and chlorinated solvent risk, while maintaining salinity at a level acceptable for the intended irrigation crops and soils.
secondary_goals: maintain performance under intermittent industrial shock loads
limit long-term soil metal accumulation risk
address uncertain legacy VOC occurrences with a defensible multi-barrier approach
avoid recommending a train that is overly sensitive to fluctuating cyanide toxicity
balance agricultural reuse quality with moderate budget and operator capability

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: metal-bearing sludge can be managed off-site in limited quantities; frequent carbon replacement or large brine volumes are undesirable

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_source_control_bufferin: dampen variable industrial pulses, enable safer cyanide management, and reduce shock loading to downstream biological treatment
core_unit_functions.alkaline_chlorination_or_equivalent_cont: destroy cyanide under controlled pH and oxidation conditions before biological exposure
core_unit_functions.coagulation_precipitation_for_metals_and: remove cobalt and associated particulate or hydroxide-forming metals and reduce suspended solids
core_unit_functions.biological_nutrient_removal: reduce BOD, residual COD, ammonia, and nitrogen-related irrigation impacts
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish solids and protect downstream VOC and organics polishing units
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_packed_tower_aeration_w: remove volatile chlorinated solvent mass including cis-1,2-dichloroethylene where transfer conditions are favorable
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: capture residual VOCs and other trace organics not fully characterized
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: control pathogen risk for agricultural reuse

applicability_rationale: this train directly addresses the mixed inorganic and organic profile using staged barriers rather than relying on one dominant unit process
cyanide should be managed before biological treatment because episodic toxicity could destabilize nitrification and broader biological performance
cobalt removal is reasonably aligned with precipitation-based metals control after cyanide destruction and pH adjustment
air stripping plus GAC provides a risk-aware response to confirmed cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and suspected related VOC uncertainty
the train better matches agricultural reuse than desalination-heavy options because TDS is elevated but not so high that RO is automatically justified for all crop scenarios
equalization is essential because the influent is explicitly variable and partially characterized
potential_risks: cyanide oxidation must be tightly controlled to avoid incomplete destruction or formation of undesirable oxidant byproducts, especially under variable industrial chemistry
air stripping effectiveness for cis-1,2-dichloroethylene depends on temperature, Henrys-law behavior, and off-gas treatment design; site piloting is advisable
if cobalt is present in strong complexes, precipitation performance may be lower than expected and polishing may be needed
other chlorinated VOCs are suspected but not fully characterized, so GAC empty-bed contact time and replacement frequency remain uncertain
salinity and chloride may still restrict use on salt-sensitive crops even if contaminant removal is successful

core_unit_functions.equalization: buffer variable influent quality and flow
core_unit_functions.cyanide_oxidation: reduce acute cyanide toxicity before biomass contact
core_unit_functions.metal_precipitation_and_high_rate_clarif: remove cobalt and lower solids loading
core_unit_functions.MBR: provide strong BOD, ammonia, and solids removal with compact high-quality effluent
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish residual VOCs and trace organics
core_unit_functions.UV_or_chlorine_disinfection: ensure pathogen control for reuse application

applicability_rationale: MBR can provide stable low-turbidity effluent that is attractive for irrigation reuse distribution systems
the approach is compact and robust for municipal-organic loading while still allowing front-end control of cyanide and cobalt
GAC provides a flexible polishing barrier where VOC composition is not completely known
this option may outperform conventional activated sludge on solids polishing and pathogen precursor reduction
potential_risks: membrane fouling risk rises if front-end metals and oxidant residual control are inconsistent
MBR biology remains vulnerable if cyanide spikes bypass or overwhelm the oxidation step
GAC alone may be insufficient for higher-than-expected VOC peaks or unknown co-contaminants
operator skill and maintenance demand may exceed a medium-complexity preference during upset conditions

core_unit_functions.equalization: stabilize variable influent and allow controlled dosing
core_unit_functions.cyanide_oxidation: destroy cyanide prior to downstream biological exposure
core_unit_functions.coagulation_precipitation_and_clarificat: remove cobalt and reduce suspended and colloidal matter
core_unit_functions.ozone_or_UV_peroxide_as_contingency_poli: provide additional oxidation capacity for partially characterized trace organics where adsorption alone may be insufficient
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb and biologically polish residual dissolved organics
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: meet microbial expectations for agricultural reuse

applicability_rationale: this is a defensible multi-barrier option when the utility wants an explicit response to evolving standards for trace organics
advanced oxidation can serve as a contingency where VOC and unknown organics profiles shift over time
biological activated carbon offers broader polishing than conventional filtration alone
potential_risks: advanced oxidation is less directly efficient for all chlorinated solvent scenarios than physical removal and may not be the most economical primary control for cis-1,2-dichloroethylene
oxidation chemistry under variable bromide or organic matrix conditions may create byproduct-management questions
process tuning is more complex and may be difficult without detailed characterization of the emerging contaminant profile
this option may over-treat average conditions while still requiring pilot verification for peak events

core_unit_functions.cyanide_oxidation: protect biology and lower cyanide concentration
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce TDS, metals, and many dissolved contaminants to produce high-quality reclaimed water

applicability_rationale: RO is a technically strong polishing barrier if regulators or crop plans become highly restrictive for salinity and trace contaminant exposure
the train offers substantial contaminant removal margin under uncertainty
it may become attractive if future standards tighten or if salt-sensitive crops dominate reuse demand
potential_risks: this option is likely over-conservative for current average water quality and conflicts with the stated sensitivity to energy and residuals
brine management is a major unresolved issue for agricultural reuse facilities with limited concentrate disposal options
membrane fouling or oxidation residual carryover can increase operational fragility
RO does not remove the need for robust front-end cyanide control and source monitoring

core_unit_functions.conventional_activated_sludge: remove biodegradable organics under standard municipal treatment conditions

applicability_rationale: included as a lower-ranked baseline to show that standard municipal treatment alone is not sufficient for this mixed contaminant scenario
it may address routine BOD and solids reduction but does not provide targeted control for cyanide, cobalt, or chlorinated solvent uncertainty
potential_risks: cyanide shock loads could inhibit biological treatment and cause compliance instability
cobalt removal would be unreliable and may permit gradual soil accumulation risk under irrigation reuse
cis-1,2-dichloroethylene would not be adequately addressed by this train
conventional treatment without targeted polishing is poorly aligned with evolving standards and legacy contamination concerns

preferred_rank_pattern: treatment trains with explicit cyanide pretreatment should rank above trains that expose biology directly to uncertain cyanide pulses
multi-barrier trains addressing both VOC uncertainty and cobalt removal should rank above conventional municipal-only approaches
equalization should appear in higher-ranked options because influent variability and episodic wet-weather VOC intrusion are central to the case
desalination-heavy options may appear but should not automatically rank first because agricultural reuse need is crop- and salinity-specific rather than universally requiring RO

must_reflect: legacy solvent contamination is episodic and not fully characterized, so recommendations should include uncertainty-aware polishing or contingency logic
mixed organic-inorganic contamination requires staged treatment rather than single-process solutions
agricultural reuse requires discussion of pathogens, salinity, and long-term soil metal implications
energy and residuals constraints should temper enthusiasm for RO or highly chemical-intensive treatment

must_contain: why cyanide should usually be controlled before biological treatment
why cobalt is commonly handled through precipitation or related metal-removal chemistry
why VOC management may need air stripping, adsorption, or another dedicated barrier rather than relying only on conventional biology
why simple municipal treatment is insufficient under uncertain industrial and legacy contamination conditions

should_mention: uncertainty about related chlorinated VOCs beyond cis-1,2-dichloroethylene
possible cyanide toxicity shocks to nitrification and biomass stability
cobalt speciation or complexation uncertainty affecting precipitation performance
off-gas handling needs if air stripping is recommended
salinity and chloride impacts on crop suitability even if contaminant targets are met
tradeoffs between stronger contaminant barriers and increased sludge, carbon replacement, or brine burdens
